speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Elmer Wells came west from North Carolina in 1902 and settled down to work in and around Moscow. In this interview, the subjects are helping Moscow banks during the Depression. He is sent east to survey the situation and runs $200 ,000 in bonds to Spokane, chicanery surrounding the Harding presidency, the fall of Woodrow Wilson, testifying on behalf of Will, no fishing allowed in Moscow's muddy streets, and Republicans dominate Idaho.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: There wasn't very many homes in Moscow east of Polk Street, I guess about six or eight homes is all. And this side there wasn't too many homes. Business building, a lot of them here at the time, right at Moscow Hotel, several other places, and I got a job working in the hardware and information store where Pennie's store is at now. That is way back in 1903.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you come here? Was that when you first came in 1903?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: No, I come here for 1902.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And so I've been here ever since, you know. I know a lot of people, worked on the ranches around here at Old Farm, worked for other people on the ranches, run the ranches for them. So I've been here a very long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the store like when you first went into it to work at any store?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Whether it was larger and the implements that you was storing up. And I worked in there for MG Shield. You own a lot of land in different places around here, farms, you know, that time too.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Some of my folks came out here and they decided to buy some land out here towards Moscow Mountain. They did farm out there for 15 years, decided to quit that coming down here, hired out to Washburn Wilson. He was going to start the P business down here Monday morning. I hired out to him. I did work for him 17 years and a half.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And I got all that trucking and everything. And so I decided to take it a little easier so I went out working on the ranches, you know, helping that way. And then I got tired of that and I couldn't work entirely.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: So I've been idle, have done the thing for a long period of time. And I've been sitting here at the hotel a long time. I've been here at the same place here a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me about some of the things that happened in the early days that were pretty colorful around Moscow?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Really. Yeah.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yes, just before I come here, there's a man who lives out east towards the cemetery, out along the old road, where that blacksmith's shop is. He lives like that place. But he went on a rampage of some kind, killed two or three of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this Stephens?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah, right here in Moscow. And there's surrounding this place out there in all the sheets, there's a curtain shooting at some of the windows and that sort of thing. They finally killed him anyhow. That's before I come here. And he had the other troubles coming up in all this. Pretty good, pretty strong. Some of the other things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me some other ones besides that one?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That one I've heard of because that's a pretty famous one. You'd heard of that. That, he killed, that guy was Stephens, was his name. Yeah, I know.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: So I worked around on the ranch, that ranch out here, between here and the cemetery. I worked on it three years for the people of the Zondes and worked on the others besides that, around here, and besides that, quit that entirely. I did. So I decided I could make my home here at the hotel and I just stayed here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was told that there was a trial that you testified in once. It was really funny. It was great. Everybody thought it was great.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember that? You know what I'm talking about?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, yes. I'll tell you what that was. It was Egan's apartment. You know where the Al Drugstore was at and the hardware store there. It connects them. That belonged to Egan's, Mrs. Egan. She had two children. I knew the old man four. When she passed away, he had a lawsuit over his two children, the daughter and him. I was the one who testified. I was right there all the time and knew what went on. So I was the last one to testify and put all the others on beforehand until she meets the very last. When it did, I really told them. They had the goods, dates, and everything. The case went against him, of course.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He met his sister and he figured something might happen to him. He had made a will giving his portion to the city youth. You know, something like that, too. After that building was set, on East Thursday, we had something like that. You know, where he sat down there. He built that, both of them built that. But the suit went against him, against her. He got off of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that you had to say that turned it against him?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, it was in the garden he was drinking and hurling. Drinking, you know, both.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Mostly his hurling, you know. I told him right out and out, you know. The whole last one. One witness was sure that when he made that will and so forth, that he talked to him and he was drunk and didn't know what he was doing. That man was the biggest liar that ever lived, and the attorney for her had found him. So, when that man got to me, I understand, of course, that he come and talked to me. It was my room and told me what he had done. Half an hour before, I want to know what conditions he had. I told him he just told me he was a judge. When the whole house is ruined.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And the judge had taken off with his lady, his type. She had just laid down the land and got him strong enough. I guess she thought she had seen him a few times when he was a judge too. Well, they had rooms right here at the hotel, you know. I found out that you had come to the verdict. The opponent down told me he was going up to the courthouse to find out. And I went over to the house and let them know down until they went up there and found out too, you know. Found out how everything went. And they finally sold the property to 110 ,000. They each got 55 ,000 piece. And I said, I was caught up with it and they were losing it. The case entirely.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she had said that he, how was he related to her?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: No relation. No relation, whatever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why should she have gotten the money in the first place? How? Why?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, how would she have gotten the money if there was no relation to the...

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: When there was two old lady agents, had two children. She's one of them and he was one. She had two, a boy and a girl. Now, you see. And so he passed away and she contested his will, you know. And he ran against him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who did his will leave it to? Did he leave it all to that youth center?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah, that's what he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she said that he was a drinker and you said that he was sober as a judge and that she was a drinker.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yes, you know. And then asked me about her. I told her about it being drunk and pounding on the door. So she went and killed her, slatting the door. She was drunk. She said, what the devil is she doing?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: So that's the way that went, you know. And so I've been here a long time. There's lots of trials coming up in court. I was on a jury which got along all right to that, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, and is there another good case that you can remember when you was on the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any cases when you were on the jury that came up?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, it was not too much of a cause. And most of the cases aren't too big around here. No, there wasn't at that time either. No, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I heard you weren't too welcome in the place after that, after she lost the case. I had room at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when I testified that morning, and when I come down to go up in the room, and they asked me, couldn't I find me another place to stay?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I told her she needed to say the goddamn word. I already had it ready to go here. Had some pockets right here now to keep with the room. She needed to say the damn word. And after all, here I had to keep. She already had a place to go to. She was going to hell.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And here, lately, I had a birthday. I don't know how, but during the president didn't find a family. Sent me a card.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Took up homesteads, you know. Consequently, he didn't have very much money. He did the homework, you know. You understand?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He had to. So it didn't go very good for quite a long while there, you know. For that matter. Probably got around, so it didn't hold a lot of difference, you know. The wheat wasn't very good, but price, it wasn't doing very good. Later on, later, the land was pretty cheap. The High Street Bank in Spokane had a mortgage on, oh, 20 different ones in this county. And they had advertised for sale and had a little book put out like a small telephone. A book used to have. And they described the places, how much ground there was and about the watering, and so forth. And then they had over $17 an acre. Now it's over, the some of them sold already, $300 an acre.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was Moscow a pretty poor town, or was there a fair bit of wealth in Moscow, compared to the rest of the county, or what was it like?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, there was early, you know, and things that, I don't know, just how to describe it, you know. Things wasn't going too good, and he had potlots. People decided to build a mill over here, you know. That helped out a great deal. It did a lot of work to a lot of people. That time, you know, it hasn't only for yet, you know, for that matter. It's been a good help to this county, because they very wealthy bunch, you know.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: They owned so many, so much timberland in a number of different states, you know. And one out here, you know, a few years ago, when I first came out, I happened to be in a bank over at Tacoma. It was run across the manage, I knew that. And I talked to a few, several times down, when I lived down at Ashwood North Carolina. So I surprised to see him. He surprised me, to see me. So he asked me, how did I stand out here?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I told him, if you couldn't, I'd come out and look over. If I liked his ass, he didn't. I could always go back. So I asked him, he said, he's here in Vesta, Georgia. It doesn't have to be a lot of money. And he, the state, the city of Washington, had a lot of timberland, chief timberman, he needed the money bed, and he was buying up big trucks up there above Kelso, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where is it?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: This mountain, capital was our snow -capped mountain, I don't know what's the name of it. Now, that small mountain, the old journey. But then he bought a lot of it, and he's in our painting, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the guy's name? This guy?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I'd bring him by the way, right now. Oh, it doesn't matter. Yeah. I just don't know. And he said, it's cheap timberland, but the time's going to be when it'd be very wealthy. And if person had money to invest, it'd be all right. So he bought a lot of it, and it helped pay taxes. Our taxes wasn't much for a few years, and then it sold us out to warehouse people, you know. And he went to the Longview, down in the boat, cropped in, started building Longview, bring it down there in Houston.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And all the wealthy, wealthy here a couple years ago, they bought out another company down in Arkansas, North Kansas. It's a lot of timberland, and two mills. They gave him $30 million for it, that's all.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: So, you know, that's a lot of money. To fortune. More than that. To fortunes.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: That's the reason. Down in the actual North Carolina, west of the actual 15 miles. Good size large place, for that matter. A lot of nice hotels there, as we use any word, in the west, any word.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made you decide to come out from there?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, I think after the Civil War is over with, the things never could go very good for the south there for years, you know. It was not too good, and wages wasn't very heavy. And so I decided to come with to find out how it went out here. So I come to see that one, and he's right over there in the west. I said that could go to hell, and I come over here, and it went pouring rain every minute.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I'd like to look over here, and I stay here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you send for your parents, or did they come out with you?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah, folks know me so. Out back there, and come here, and we bought some land out here, and farmed for a long time. And then Mother and Brother decided they wanted to go to California, and sold out, and they went down to California, and I wouldn't go. I'd go down there and visit them, but I wouldn't stay there at all. And he bought us Ontario, 38 miles east of Los Angeles, and out there, he had the largest counties to use, and the whole United States, San Bernardino County, you know. And I didn't like it down there. Too much of different nationalities, like Japs, and Chinese, and niggers, and Mexicans, anything, everything you would ever think of, you know. You could be down there on the beach, on the pipe down there in two days' time, and see people from every nationality on earth go by, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that why you didn't like it?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yes, I didn't like it at all. My eyes wasn't made to look such a thing. So I gave it to you to buy. You wouldn't stay there at all. Came back up. Yeah, I was sure, sure, yeah. Yeah, I made all the tools. I went down there, 12 different trips, you know. And I knew so many people go over to Arizona, and go over there and visit the while, and they go on down, for a visit down there, and then go on down to North Carolina and George's Beach and come home, man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you must have been about 20 by the time you came out here from North Carolina, right?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I was 22. 22.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, can you tell me a little bit of what it was like in North Carolina when you were a young man, and trying to make a go of it before you decided to come?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, at that time, anything you can raise down south had a very low price, and everybody wasn't doing too good, you know. You can't do it any better. You know, when you've got raisin tobacco, and cotton, cotton, corn, and so forth, it just wasn't doing very good. The prices wasn't any good, you know. You have to keep raising, you know, to try to keep living.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You came out here all by yourself, you didn't come?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: No, no, there's a bunch of coming, and I come along with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, was a bunch of your friends people coming in?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah, yeah, five of us come. Yeah. One's talking to Colorado, he's going to stay there, and the other's, all the bounty stuff went on to Seattle.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And some of them got a job in the shipyards over in the marand shipyard, and that's when my brother was going to stay there. Never stopped, just to train all the time, he never stopped to rein the dome.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did, were these guys that came out, did you all know each other back in North Carolina?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh, yes, oh, yes.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh, yes, there were boys around there.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah, I know people all over the country who never were else, and then I,

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: they'd run quite a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So tell me, tell me some more about the early days when you first got here, about some things that happened in Moscow, and what the...

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: There wasn't too much happening, it was just a farm in town, you know, so Main Street is muddy, and down here where the theater's at, where there's a big hole in the pavement area, and a street down there, water's there, and signs, no fishing allowed.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And attacked the hotel, Moscow Hotel, it goes to the depot to meet packages and bring them back, and it got stuck, and they had to get out and walk.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I was talking to some of the boys over at the David store, and they could come but they'd come in, and I got a room at Moscow Hotel, took a look at the hotel, the store here, David and Elan, and some of them made up their mind, and I guess they'd buy me a suit closer, and I think I did buy one. I told them that I'd been buying from... ever since.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Take my records of that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'm just starting to now, because I want to know what you told the people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd you tell them about Wilson?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, he'd decided to go to the Peace Conference, you know. Well, he went, and I said that'd be the worst thing he could do. He better stay away from it. I'll tell you why. Those angry people in France over there, they had long -headed skippers, you know, getting me into something that was the only big thing, he's the whole darn thing, getting spoofed up. And I said, I don't know what to do, but what I looked for, England and France both were having a lot of trouble with their colonies, and they wanted the United States to join in with them, if they could get to do that in some way or another, if not to help the colonies. Now, Dean helped the others. They entertained him over there, with General Wilson, and they'd come out and the people would say, I don't know what the good name of the people, but their names are League of Nations.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I said, well, they named my baby for it. He gives the goodest name, I said, to the League of Nations. And I said, now, he'll come back here, the press here is already against it. And I said, you watch what's coming up. I've already told you now what's going to happen. He'll campaign for it, speak for it. He won't make many talks until he breaks down. When he wasn't here very long, he decided to go out and speak for it. He spoke to San Francisco, and he got such an ungodly cold reception, right?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He almost slapped his face for it. Well, he left and went over to Pueblo, Colorado, and started to talk there, and that's where he fell over. His hands just fell over to one side.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And of course, he took care and put him on trains, took him back east, to be sure. And to be certain that they didn't let anyone see him anymore.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: The men in the cabinet had claimed that he'd seen or found out enough that he wasn't in his right mind in very bad shape, you know. So he called the cabinet together. And when he did, the widowers, doctor, went to him, and I never knew the name of it. He called it right to him. Anyhow, he told him that his mind is good, and no one had the right to call the cabinet together except the president himself. And he asked him to this band immediately if not he'd pawn a new cabinet before the summit. This bandage, of course, you would too, wouldn't you?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: This face slapped for his head right. Now, Woodrow Wilson was in that shape, and no one ever knows. There's a lot of losses passed, and his wife evidently signed him. Probably he didn't know anything. And you're going under those laws yet, of course. And it's obviously Dr. Grayson, who was his doctor, and he'll never tell you, of course. His own wife would never tell him. He never knew. He just had to make a good, long, big guess, don't you know, wouldn't you think so? Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know anything about when you first came out here, about what the politics was like in Idaho?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I know there was a bunch of things going on then. There's a strong, you can smell them from miles, Republicans. Oh, strong Republicans, you know. Next door, the Republican bunch, you know, down here, governors and everything else. In case there did, the Democrats got into it, we ain't got anyone now, you know, and you see right now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was there a lot of populism going on in Idaho then? Didn't they?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard that. They called it populism, and I don't know too much about it.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh, they had a lot of stuff going, something like this, you know. It didn't last very long, you know. Just made fun of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about when Steunenberg got shot?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yeah. Well, he was governor, you know, when he had the trouble up here in the Coeur d'Alene mines, and he went out in a mold, putting camp up there. Everything was rested, putting camp. A lot of people didn't have anything to do with it. Made a lot of them mad, but he finally got out of his governor and decided to kill him. That's just what he did, and he had his comment. That night, he shot and killed him, and he did a good job, and they killed him before he did.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: What about the labor troubles in the— That's what we had at that time. Well, I was thinking more about the IWW troubles around World War I around here. I heard they threw a bunch of guys in a bullpen in Moscow here. Oh, they did, yeah. They arrested a few of them, you know. There was a little trouble around here, you know. Sure, which way to get the hell away from here. They went.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: So I had to have a little trouble to have a little fun occasionally, you know. Anyway, it got along pretty good, you know, the things were running pretty good, you know, in some ways, don't you know?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Not too bad. Compared to other places, you know. It got along here better during the Depression than any place you could find yourself in the United States. How it got along?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, found out it was coming, and so they went preparing for it, you know, and then it turned up. It hit pretty strong, and Roosevelt was elected. Franklin Roosevelt was elected, and he had took Roosevelt off for a shift, and he tried to kill him down at Tampa, Florida, you know. And so then he took the oath to office on Saturday, and he ordered all banks, United States, clothes until they could find out what shape they seemed, whether they could be saved or not, you know. In the windup, there's 8 ,500 banks closed. You know what that was mean?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: A lot of people were never able to pay more than 25 and 35, $0 .04 on a dollar bank. 8 ,500.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I knew the Arizona Airborne clothes, and most of the states had quite a number of clothes, and they had a hard time getting them off. The idea was that the money wasn't circulating at all, and Teddy, the money man, sat down on Teddy, and gave him 24 hours to turn that money loose. If not, he printed down much money, there wouldn't be one of the continental dams. They turned it loose, too.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And there's very bad shapes in them, and things just wasn't going too good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said that around here, the people got along really good then.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Yes, they did. For 10 days from work here, the Moscow River, it couldn't get any use back east at all. It just closed up, wasn't telling them anything. They wanted to find out something, so they sent me back there to find out. They were a national bank, Spokane and this bank here. We need to get a Sydney bank. I did an awful lot of things running around, inquiring all the way before Chicago, Milwaukee, and Chicago, and Terre -O -Hale and Orson. Oh, it's a lot of other places. I was all over down there. So I don't know what goddamn condition they were seeing back there. I told them to watch out. There's going to be an awful lot of banks closed. There's certainly one other. When they got their word across, they made up the mind that they sent me for that business. My god, they were going to do what I told them to do. So when they wanted to come in for a bank, wanted to run it for 30 days, 9, 60, 90, they couldn't show them that they had the money on that day to pay it back, they would have let them have it. That's what saved them. If they hadn't been that way, they'd have been tied up too, you know. It was not very bad. Three years, it had seemed damn bad shape here. It was not pretty bad. Well, old tues, Spokane, the phone down thing, K .O. here, I don't know. He'd been caught in the jam, maybe, you see. They wanted them to send up $200 in bonds, $200 ,000. What do you call it?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: A deep bond. 20 just left. Ten minutes before. I had been, about ten minutes later, 10, 15 minutes. I hadn't talked to him until I was going up to Spokane for two or three days, said, don't leave till I talk to you again. We went around there and he and Melgert had a talk and I told him I had some money. I tried to get what they were saying, but I didn't tell him. Melgert said, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, we'll just do that. He went back in and banked there. Melgert came out and he and I talked. He finally came out, got them to build it about that long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that long?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: The reason we got that long, two or twice, was $200 ,000 in bonds, sending up to an old -fashioned bank to do it. I remember how the bank spoke at. He went over to the windows there and asked which one is the most right here. The foreign aid one bank here. That one. So he pointed out, I went over there and told him who I were. I was down here in Moscow. When he formed down to have him send him up the bonds, the train had been left 10 to 15 minutes. So he got a great instant by me. I pulled it out to hand it to him and he stunned him so damn bad. He had been there for two before he could reach out and get it. So he told the hands off for it. He and I went over to the exchange department. He told him here's those bonds he'd formed down for him. He looked at the bonds and then he looked at me and he looked at the bonds again and then he looked back at me. He turned around, I don't know how long I knew these. I said, I told 20 odd years. They'd do the most instant if you wouldn't. I said, that's the one that's taking a lot of territory. He said, I mean it to help money. I said, oh, here, here's all I want. He said, let me tell you something. I got two men working here in this bank that I chose to send. It was bonds for them down to my school. I said, yeah, I've got very good men working here. That's for ages to about good. I said, no. I said, hey, call him up and let him know you got them. So he called on me. He called. He came and said, I got a shock man to go and wear it. I showed up. I knew you would. He said, he'd bring it just the same for either one of us. He said, that's what he said. He said, you know, I think I'll keep him up here. I'd use him. He said, no, you won't. We'll use him here. We won't let him go.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, I think probably there's a little trouble up there. About 200 ,000, wouldn't you say?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He'd get a hold of him and he'd borrow money with them, you know. But the old national clothes here, it's still for a couple of months. And they'd throw it just down here for a few days. And they wouldn't let anyone have any money but $10 until they sent up a Spokane and they told them who is trying to back, which one they sent with that money down here. When they got it down here, plenty of money, they told them they'd have all the money they wanted. Now they didn't want any more. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You worked for the old national, right? Down here?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: No, I didn't work for each one of the banks. But they sent me back there to find out for them, yeah. And you sent me on other trips too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who were you carrying the money for? Which bank did you carry the money for when you carried the bonds? Which bank were you carrying the bonds for?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, it was this bank right over here, down here on the corner. Oh, the first national. No, it was the... Oh, the, yeah. Yeah. I don't know what you mean. Yeah, the other bank. Yeah, the Idaho First, yeah. Yeah, that's one. Yeah. They were then afraid to trust me and send it by me and Spokane. And got it. And so it was eight farmers around here. And one man over here, a lot of acres and a good crop of broil. And he just wanted to offer us nine dollars a ton. So he wanted a thousand dollars for the bank. He wanted to borrow the machines he really needed. He went into the bank and I asked him about it. So they told him, he told him, no, by the way, don't tell him to sell. And I said, I'm always a man. You run the bank successfully. I run the farm successfully. I don't care about someone telling me how I should run my business if he don't want to let it go. By God's sake, so he says, no, sell. So he said, I'll let you have it. Well, that's all right. He said, well, we almost had a little cuss fight. So he wanted to have a return that he wanted to congratulate him on having a sense of success. And he said, thank you for not being closed up like yours. He says, you were here. I've got a letter on his ring. He went back and I got my letter brought out in the ring. He said, by God's name, we're in the third year and we don't see any pickup yet. He says, he's going to run his five years. He said to him, he says, you darn know. He says, now, that letter saves us from being closed up. Right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me again, just what you put in that letter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you say in that letter?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: What did I say?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah. What did you tell him that saved him from being closed up?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh, I told him it's going to be an awful lot of, you know, back East. Yeah. I told him that off the condition is he just couldn't imagine how bad it really was. It's just so much worse than what you think. He just couldn't tell us. It's just no way to figure it out hard. I said, you look out for three years from bed. He looked like to me to go run five years in such a damnable shape back here. And he worked too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you told him just be real careful, huh?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I told him yes. He said, be damn careful. I told him to go to the book. Be damn sure yours is not one of them. He wouldn't let it be.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Then he had his mortgage on the goddamn place in California. He settled it down there. So it sent me down there. I didn't know what that was. Went down there to get the money so high I started to tell him. So they told me yes. They sent it up too much as no. He sent me here to bring it up myself. They wouldn't trust that other bank down there to save him from hell.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And they had a lot of trouble, you know. Down in Georgia over there, their bank closed down there. And a lot of others, a lot of them, in my relation, had a lot of money in the bank. And I knew it was going to hurt to be banned. And he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come the Bank of Troy did some good? Do you know why?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard the Bank of Troy did real good in the Depression as far as staying on its feet.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: They did take a lot of money, pockets of people, you know, and run. You know they got over $16 billion on deposit?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Lots of doing over that bank, you know, and getting on the alright now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about some of the guys, some of the old people that were well known in Moscow in the early days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said that you knew McConnell?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me what he was like?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, I don't know. The only thing I knew he was getting quite old, you know, and he had great desire to read a great deal. And I did too, and we knew each other, and he gave me reading the matter, you know, and so forth. And so, you know, you know, a little bit of a borer, you know. She was one of his daughters, you know.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: So he got along alright. So anyway, when...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let's see now, who was this?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He was nominated for President. He wanted a borer to go around and talk for him. The borer told him he would if he agreed to certain things. Well, that was old Taft. If he agreed to certain things, like he said he'd have to fight for the party he would. The borer went around and talked to him. He went down to Ashford, North Carolina to give him a talk down there. And the women folks there at the hotel, it was entertaining, Mrs. Borer. And the lady up to her, Mrs. Borer told him that she lived in Moscow. So the lady from the hotel there said why she had a sister who lived here in Moscow. Ask her the name. She said, oh, she says, we know her well. She says, probably when he was great friends. She said, yeah, you betcha.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And then I've heard a lot of this here being nominated for President and so forth. Here several years ago there's three. I don't remember the names. Don't make it much different. There's Ron for that office. And the big money man in New York. And then, didn't want either one. So they got a hold of the man in the Republican Party that had charges looking after things. They told him, you know, he didn't want either one of them. Whatever. So he done what they told him to do. He had a secret meeting at St. Louis and called him up there to get started. And told him what was what. He didn't want either one of those.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And when he opened the window, he had quite a lot of talk amongst himself, who in the devil did he nominate now? The whole delegation said, why not Harding? He was a governor and said that he'd done anything and everything they wanted him to do. If he's crooked, he's all right too. That's just very wonderful. When they went back and told Harding, is he's going to get the nomination?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: It was already made up that he had two ballots. Not vote very much for either one of those three. And some states passed without voting at all. And then third ballots, third nominations come up. And the president was going to nominate Harding, and he gets many of the calls that can be made unanimous. I'm going to let him know he's going to get anything. The whole delegation did. Well, anyway, Old Dick Harder, who's down in Toulouse, Oklahoma, had married Harding's sister. He had been living away from her and apart from her for five years. He'd quit her and was living as a whore as I did. And this little woman had all kinds of money. Had four or five wheels going. Well, she took a liking to it and she adopted him. Took him into her own house, bought him clothes and made a man out of him. She thought so much of him. She gave him one of those wheels that was produced in Bork Brown. And it soon struck wheels on those. He was doing pretty damn good, you know. So he was producing oil. But Harding wrote to him and told him, he knew, he had a good standing with an older man, knew him well. And he let them know that he wanted them to raise $100 ,000 for his campaign. And he'd do whatever they wanted to ask from them. Well, God, they would raise that in a few days. I don't see them. I was old, old Hayden. Them was right on the spot, you know. So they raised that $100 ,000 right away. Well, anyway, then he got the second letter from Harding. He told him he wanted to put him in the cabinet. And he couldn't and wouldn't put him in unless he would leave his woman and go back to leaving with his family. He wouldn't work otherwise. He'd have to go back to his family. Well, he got the second letter. So he told his city that he was going back to leave with his quit and he was going back to family. She wouldn't stand for his talk. She didn't have a damn thing when he'd come. And I was pretty well fixed and every daughter he had, she'd give it to him. She wasn't going to stand for it. But he started packing up for things to go. She took it. He probably killed him right in the wrong house. He jumped in the car. She always cared a lot of money on it. He went out to the airport, told him that she wanted...

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Are you interested in that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, just recording.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: She went to the airport, told him she wanted to make a quick trip to Fort Worth and back, filled it up. Well, they did. They did that before. When they got in the air, she pulled a gun on him, so he wasn't going for it. He was going down to Mexico. He was in the El Dorado town, first one below El Paso over in Mexico. That's where he was going. And I landed down there and she told him why. She had these letters and she used to go to the exposed Republican Party for the whole time to come and send some more. Well, when they got back, they found out where he was at and where she was at. One of these oilmen there, two of them, and the prosecutor turned it... There were three of them. This plane went down there to see her. When they gave her so much money to get possession of those goddamn letters, then the promister, she could come back to tell us and they went and prosecuted for killing old Jake Carter.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And she did that. Well, Hardin was elected, of course. Jake Carter was dead. He couldn't put him in. The oilmen had put up $100 ,000 for his campaign fund.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: He let's it up to them to name who they wanted. They named Albert Paul. And he owned a lot of acres in New Mexico.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This, now you're getting into the teapot dome, aren't you?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: You betcha I'm going to that. Yeah. Anyhow, he owned a lot of Atlanta. Well, anyway, Hardin had to take it out of one division, put his name under another, so he'd be under Albert Paul's jurisdiction. You understand?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And he did that right here. Then Albert Paul went to bits and let them start hitting on a piece of teapot dome, let them have it, let them keep a gale keel, all of a sudden, you know, in California. You'd know about that, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you tell me what I should know because I don't know much about it. Yeah. Well, the two of them, he had them have teapot dome in Montana, you know.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And they said it cost each one of them $66 ,000 apiece for the gale keel. Albert Paul had $100 ,000 and it was a campaign, you know. He said it cost them $66 ,000 and they figured it was worth $5 million. He thought, do it. Well, the ship caught a lot of oil out of that, but it had people in the wilder, and truly had record of it, of course. And here,

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: they shipped a lot of oil. But anyway, teapot dome scandals broke open.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: And what it did was, here, they hardened, you see, hardened past the wind, and Cootie took over, you know. But it broke. Well, he hadn't been in the office for a few days. There was teapot dome scandal that showed up. When he called the Senator Borer up from Idaho, and when I talked to him, Borer went over course. Cootie wasn't a man who talked very much. Never did very much anything. So he told him why he called him for it. He had two cabinet members. He didn't hedge it from the Hardin's administration. He satisfied his guilty ship from bribes and all the ships. He says, yes, I agree with you on that. What would you do if you was in my place?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Oh, so that's what you want to know. I kick him out, say, quick, put a long deal. Take the hats with him. So that's what you want to know. He says, I'll just do that. I'm going to let you see me do it. So he called up Albert Fallow, turned over. He told us, make out his listeners friend. He told him to sign his listeners. I didn't call you over to the reg. He was trying to leave us alone. He said, you're already out of office. Well, you might know he was shot gone. I don't know. He told us, he called for the other ones to come up to come in.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I said, what's his name right now? I've got it down.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: What he called him over and told him to sign. He started to generate. He's making them different. He don't have to sign. You don't have to leave it alone, but you're already out of office. So those two were out. They tried four of them for that and sent them long prison sentences. One committed suicide rather than sent to the pen for a long period of time. And old Doheny on the Hill of Orange Reserve in California, tried him twice, but they knew that they turned loose each time the jury would get a couple thousand dollars apiece under the table. And they turned loose most of the time. And that's the way they went with the whole goddamn works.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think about this kind of stuff? Do you think that was just wrong or terrible, or is that just the way the government was, or what do you think of all this stuff?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Well, they were just rotten pieces. There were some of them right there, you know. Or Harding who else, you know. One man, I don't remember his name right now. It's the man who must have me think of it. But he had the job of going around over the United States, selecting a place to be able to fetch hospitals. And he was carrying some of those men's wife along with him all the time. And drunk frequently. Well, before he got all two, and he was right here in Spokane, and the Coeur d 'Alene people came out and got him and took him up on the river, showing him around, trying to get Bill to go on his Coeur d 'Alene. And he said, don't keep almost them out of the boat and goddamn it. Well, anyway, he got back to Washington DC and let him go. And he said he had done a damn bit worse than the others, and then said, old Harding was down there on, that was a red house on K Street. That's where they'd meet down there and gamble for money, and said, old Harding had been down there, and on two different occasions, he said drunk, they wouldn't take him home to his own car, and they took him into a potassium. He had done a goddamn bit worse than the others were doing, and he didn't lie about that either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Elmer, what do you think of the Watergate?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I'd better not say anything, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'm just curious, because all this other corruption that you're talking about in government.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: It looks like, we know for certain, there's a lot of damn lying going on there. Has to be. You'll find out more of it when you start up the Watergate investigation again. I've been, like my niece Down in Fort Worth, had a letter from her yesterday. She said she heard everything he had to say when she was not, and she didn't believe it during the working day. Papers have been telling around that over 50 % of the people thinks Nixon lied. And, you know, he's telling me that the last 10 days or so, he's aged 10 years or more, aged badly, almost.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I shouldn't say this. You know, some people can understand so much, with Joe Wilson, you know, how he went. Could it be such a thing, possible that this could happen here, were there things that they're suffering to be into in the United States right now? Just hope to God he don't.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: What would you say?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I say, I don't know what's going to happen.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: No, I hope he's able to go on through with it. So if you turn him out, don't you?

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I don't know if I do or not. Where the others... I'm sorry, first thing you think was it, they'd give me a pen and say, that was Hobnob and Richard Nixon now.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: I feel sorry for him anyway. I don't know about what he's going through with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what's so interesting is that these things you're talking about back in the old times, in old history, reminds me of this. It's like the same sort of problems. You've got corruption. They had it all the time.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: The pen working out, the finding...

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Albert Ford, Mark Cannon, Teddy Roosevelt, Father -in -law, and it's private and all tools. Bought a lot of stock this year, bankrupt, company, you know, French company to try to build a canal. He's got a gift on it, back to the... And, well, they told Teddy that they had given him $50 million for it. Well, we'd see about that later. The point is, someone's secure paying the French, you know, for it. Well, they claimed this stock was very valuable. And it wind up, they stole $35 million of the... The French only got $15 million, and they already had the deal, anyway. So they divided it up. Each one of them.

speaker: Elmer Wells
words: Because... Yeah, and they divided it up there.
